problem: A right cylindrical oil tank is $15$ feet tall and its circular bases have diameters of $4$ feet each. When the tank is lying flat on its side (not on one of the circular ends), the oil inside is $3$ feet deep. How deep, in feet, would the oil have been if the tank had been standing upright on one of its bases? Express your answer as a decimal to the nearest tenth.

classified_questions.question: What is the height of the oil when the tank is lying flat on its side?
classified_questions.label: f

classified_questions.question: How can we find the area of the part of the circle covered with oil?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the ratio of the area of the oil-covered part to the entire area of the circle?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: How can we find the length of half the base of the isosceles triangle formed within the circle?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the area of the isosceles triangle?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the total area of the part of the circle covered with oil?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the fraction of the cylinder the oil covers when the tank is lying flat on its side?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the fraction of the height the oil covers when the tank is upright?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How can we determine the depth of the oil when the tank is standing upright?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the final depth of the oil when the tank is upright?
classified_questions.label: b
